text:
Alyssa Carson, age 19, “made history by mastering all of NASA's rigorous tests.” 

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Renee Porter, an astronautics researcher, reviewed NASA records and found the claim that 19-year-old Alyssa Carson mastered all of NASA’s tests for a Mars mission is incorrect. As of May 2024, Carson had not passed all NASA tests and was not part of NASA’s Astronaut Candidate Selection Program. Carson’s training occurred through a private citizens’ research group at NASA, ESA, and CSA facilities, not as a NASA astronaut candidate. Posts misstated her age; she was 23 at publication. Carson, listed as an Astronaut Candidate at the International Institute of Astronautical Sciences, is pursuing a Space and Planetary Sciences Ph.D. at the University of Arkansas, expected May 2028.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Iceland installed foldable, solar-powered shelter pods between alleyways for homeless people, as described in a November 2025 Facebook post.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Erin Caldwell, a housing researcher, examined the claim that Iceland installed foldable, solar-powered shelter pods in alleyways for homeless people. She found no credible evidence of such a project, only social posts after a November 2025 Facebook post drew tens of thousands of likes and shares and was reposted on other Facebook accounts and Instagram. She noted the description mirrors fabricated German homeless-pod stories from Fact 27. Caldwell noted small trials elsewhere. Ulm’s solar-powered Ulmer Nest has two hand-assembled prototypes under evaluation, and its Facebook page has not been updated since 2021. UK charity Amazing Grace Spaces runs limited-use emergency sleeper pods in West Bowling.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Purple apples are grown in Saskatchewan.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Mira Dalton, a digital forensics researcher, examined the claim and found the photo does not show purple-fleshed, purple-skinned apples from Saskatchewan. She confirmed the images were AI-generated after reviewing statements from the “Marian spencer” Facebook account owner and from AI artist Josh Enix, who said he created and posted them in March 2024. Dalton noted Saskatchewan orchard owner Kim McKenzie’s statement that apples grow in the province but not purple ones, with no evidence of such apples anywhere. She added that Black Diamond and Purple Bravo apples have white flesh, and the purple-fleshed star apple is not a true apple and is not reported grown in Canada.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Flocking on a preflocked Christmas tree can pose fatal health risks to pets.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. According to Dr. Livia Carver, a veterinary toxicology advisor, the claim is accurate. She reviewed toxicology references and veterinary hospital guidance and found that flocking is artificial snow, typically a plastic material. It is generally nontoxic once dry, so poisoning is not the main issue. The key hazard arises when pets ingest large amounts, creating a foreign body. Such ingestion can cause an intestinal obstruction. Without appropriate treatment, an obstruction is a medical emergency and can be fatal. Carver notes standard advice to keep pets away during application and ensure complete drying. Many inorganic substances pose similar blockage risks if eaten.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
A heart transplant recipient married his donor's wife, then died in the exact same way the donor had.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. According to Dr. Megan Porter, a medical records researcher, the claim is accurate. She reviewed public documentation from 2008 to 2010 and contemporaneous reporting. She confirmed that Sonny Graham received Terry Cottle’s heart and later married Cottle’s widow, Cheryl. Cottle died by a self-inflicted gunshot on March 15, 1995. Graham died by a self-inflicted gunshot on April 1, 2008, mirroring that method. His death was ruled a suicide in September 2009 and reaffirmed in February 2010. She noted that family members raised questions and that unresolved points about timing, method, and a message before discovery were reported. She also noted Reddit and TikTok posts that amplified the story.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
In April 2024, orange-hued images authentically depicted Athens, Greece, after it was hit by Saharan dust storm.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Claire Dawson, photo verification specialist, finds the claim that orange-hued April 2024 images show Athens after a Saharan dust storm accurate. She confirms the images are genuine news photographs, not AI-generated or altered. She notes George Vitsaras, Vassilis Psomas, and Orestis Panagiotou at EPA, Angelos Tzortzinis at AFP via Getty, and Eurokinissi via Athens Voice. AP reported orange skies over Greece from North African dust, and Greek weather service guidance, cited by the Guardian, expected clearing as winds shifted. The scale had not been seen in Athens since 2018. It spread after a Reddit post by @threafold on April 24, 2024, in r/europe, gaining over 12,000 upvotes.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
People who add salt to their food were 40% more likely to develop certain stomach cancers.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Dana Collier, a cancer epidemiologist, reviewed a peer-reviewed study published April 17, 2024 that included more than 470,000 adults who answered 2006–2010 questionnaires on adding salt. She noted that over 11 years, regular table-salt users were about 41% more likely to develop stomach cancer. She found that this association remained after considering demographic, socioeconomic, lifestyle factors, and comorbidities. She emphasized that the research was observational, so it did not establish causation and may not generalize beyond volunteers. She also observed that the study gained attention after a May 2024 Reddit r/science post and subsequent media coverage.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Astronaut Scott Kelly's heart shrank by 27% over the course of roughly one year in space.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Erin Caldwell, a cardiology researcher, examined the 2021 study and confirmed the figures. The claim that a 2021 peer-reviewed study found his heart shrank by about 27% aligns with data. She notes the measurements cover Scott Kelly’s left ventricle during his 2015–2016 International Space Station mission, not the entire heart. Left ventricular mass declined from 6.7 ounces to 4.9 ounces, averaging 0.74 grams per week. Caldwell points to a comparable weekly decline, 0.72 grams, in endurance swimmer Benoît Lecomte during a 159-day open-water swim, despite near-daily exercise. She adds that researchers described it as adaptation to reduced gravity, not dysfunction. The study analyzed two individuals.

text:
Black plastic cooking utensils are contaminated by electronic waste during a recycling process.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. The claim that black plastic cooking utensils pick up e-waste contamination during recycling is accurate. Dr. Alina Ward, a materials toxicology researcher, reviewed the 2024 peer-reviewed Chemosphere study by Toxic-Free Future and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam. She noted detections of brominated flame retardants in black plastic household items, with banned types identified. The research covered 203 Seattle purchases and applied a 50 ppm bromine screening threshold. She also underscored that black plastics are difficult to sort, increasing mixing with e-waste, and that BFRs used to reduce flammability cannot be removed once mixed. A December 2024 correction lowered an exposure estimate from 80% to 8%; contamination findings remained.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Engineers in Norway created floating artificial "ice pods" for seals to replace the animals' vanishing habitat of Arctic sea ice.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Erin Caldwell, a media analyst, evaluated a post claiming Norwegian engineers built “ice pods” for seals to replace vanishing Arctic sea ice. She found no verified evidence as of Sept. 29, 2025, and no reputable news or official announcements. She noted AI hallmarks in the image, including anomalous seal anatomy and a misrendered Norwegian flag. The image is tagged “fb@cronus.my,” which links to the Cronus Facebook page, a digital creator that posts wildlife stories with AI-like images. She found no corroboration beyond that post, and the image’s origin beyond the tag remains unverified. Sea ice loss is real and crucial for nursing, but engineered pods remain unsubstantiated.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
"1 in 2 two Mac users" are unaware of a "trick" that would allow them to clean their computer.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. According to Dr. Erin Dalton, a tech researcher, the claim 1 in 2 Mac users are unaware of a trick to clean their computer lacks support. The ad implied a hidden macOS function but redirected to MacKeeper, a legitimate Mac cleanup utility sold on monthly or annual plans. MacKeeper’s website has no data for the figure; a similar statistic is unrelated, stating, “1 in 2 of our users have had a virus before using MacKeeper.” Ads circulated since at least 2017 and were questioned on Apple Support Communities. No source or method was given. A user cited a ScanGuard ad, and the examined ad led to MacKeeper.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Cats learn to associate images with words faster than human babies do.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. The claim that a study found cats form picture–word links faster than human infants is accurate in a specific lab task. Dr. Rachel Morgan, researcher, said 31 adult cats paired animations with nonsense words and looked longer when pairings switched, indicating learned links. Infants typically need 16–20 repetitions and at least four 20‑second trials between 8 and 14 months. The task measured immediate, short‑term association only, not lasting retention or broader language understanding. Why cats form links quickly, and whether they endure or reflect understanding of word or image, remains unknown. Claim spread after the Oct. 4, 2024 Scientific Reports publication, amplified by BBC, ScienceAlert, Facebook, and X.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Scientists say an undersea volcano the size of a city off the coast of Oregon may erupt soon.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Dana Whitfield, a volcanology researcher, evaluated the claim by reviewing recent forecasts, deformation records, earthquake counts, and seafloor maps. She found it accurate. Axial Seamount, off Oregon, spans at least a dozen miles in width and length, supporting the city‑sized description. A December 2024 forecast, grounded in rising earthquakes and inflation, predicted an eruption between July 2024 and end‑2025. Similar inflation preceded eruptions in 1998, 2011, and 2015, and by April 2025 it had surpassed pre‑2015 levels without erupting. Eruptions here are typically effusive and not hazardous to people. The exact timing remains unknown. Posts on Facebook, Instagram, and X spread the claim in July 2025, including one with over 130,000 reactions.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Travelers should always keep a plastic bread bag clip in their wallet.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Kara Whitman, a travel researcher, examined the claim that travelers should always keep a plastic bread bag clip in their wallet. She reviewed a Facebook promotion in early April 2022 linking to a 90-page slideshow. One page offered a use, fixing a flip flop or sandal plug; the other 89 pages provided no support. She found no documentation or applicable travel reason for carrying a bread clip. Whitman saw Pinterest and ifunny.co posts pointing to the slideshow. She noted non-travel uses, like a makeshift guitar pick or cable organizing, which do not justify the blanket instruction. Whether a similarly titled list was linked to ads remains unclear.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
The largest reservoir of water in space is equivalent to 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Lena Hart, an astrophysicist, evaluated the claim using 2011 discovery data and peer-reviewed analyses. She confirms the largest known space water reservoir equals about 140 trillion times Earth’s ocean water. It surrounds the quasar APM 08279+5255 as vapor, not a liquid ocean, spread across hundreds of light-years. The quasar lies more than 12 billion light-years away. It is powered by a black hole about 20 billion times the Sun’s mass. Reports also note roughly 4,000 times more water vapor there than in the Milky Way. Some circulated images are artist concepts, not photos. It remains the largest and farthest-known detection.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Instagram users can see who blocked them by hitting "share" and "other."

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. According to Dr. Claire Stanton, a digital platforms researcher, the claim that Instagram users can see who blocked them by hitting “share” and “other” does not hold up. She reviewed a Meta spokesperson’s statement confirming there is no “other” button and no disclosed list of people who have blocked an account. She also considered Snopes’ observations of the app on mobile and desktop. The share arrow only offers ways to send a post to other users. “Share to” or “see all” merely expand sharing methods, including Bluetooth, Google Drive, copy link, and QR. None provide access to any blocked list.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Deep in the Gulf of Mexico exists a so-called "Jacuzzi of Despair," a pool of oceanic water that kills anything that enters it.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Megan Hartley, a marine geochemist, examined Ocean Exploration Trust’s 2015 documentation of the Gulf of Mexico brine pool and verified the details. She noted the site’s nicknames, “Jacuzzi” and “Hot Tub of Despair.” Measurements show about 100 feet in circumference, roughly 12 feet deep, nearly 3,300 feet down, and 19°C. The pool forms from salt seeping out of subsurface deposits at a cold seep where hydrocarbons escape. Its chemistry is toxic to most sea life and fatal to many animals, while adapted mussels and tube worms survive nearby. It recirculated after a January 2025 Reddit post and similar Instagram and Facebook posts. She found the claim accurate.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Dolphins have "bromances" in which two male dolphins partner up to help each other hook up with females.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. Dr. Elaine Porter, a marine mammal researcher, evaluated research since the early 1990s. She noted male bottlenose dolphins form alliances to gain and maintain mating access to females, and Dara Orbach has confirmed this behavior. She cited a 2022 PNAS study of Shark Bay, Australia Indo-Pacific bottlenose dolphins showing two to three males forming consortships with individual females lasting hours to weeks, and a 2001 Royal Society study finding males guard access to single females against rivals. Consortships can span minutes to months, with up to 13 males, and strategies vary by group and are not universal. It has circulated since 2012, including an October 2023 Reddit post.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
The Lego Group added barium sulfate to Lego bricks so they would show up on X-rays if swallowed.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Megan Hart, a materials-safety analyst, evaluated a rumor that the Lego Group added barium sulfate to bricks for X-rays. She cites a company statement saying, “LEGO bricks do not, and never have, contained any materials (either intentionally or not) to make them visible in an X-ray.” She found no evidence the company ever considered adding barium sulfate. She reviewed medical literature, including a New England Journal of Medicine report showing similar pieces are radiolucent on X-rays, consistent with other studies. She adds that barium sulfate is radiopaque and used in imaging, while typical LEGO plastics are radiolucent. The rumor dates to at least April 2020.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Japanese researchers at Kyoto University announced in December 2025 that they invented a coin-sized generator that creates 24/7 electricity "out of thin air."

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Mira Colton, an energy-systems researcher, evaluated the claim that Japanese researchers at Kyoto University announced in December 2025 a coin-sized generator creating 24/7 electricity. She reviewed Kyoto University records and found no announcement or research on a humidity-powered device. She noted the university’s 2025 work centered on solar cells. She assessed posts and identified AI-generated images, a blacked-out watermark, and clashing sizes, with content tracing to Aug. 4 and Aug. 30, 2025 Facebook pages that spread on Instagram. She cited reports that moisture-electric generators deliver power too small for practical, continuous use, and an evaporation device needs efficiency gains. No independent evidence supports rice paddy tests.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
A major ocean current in the Southern Hemisphere reversed directions for the first time in recorded history.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. The claim says a 2025 study showed a Southern Ocean current reversed direction for the first time. Dr. Erin Caldwell, an oceanographer, reviewed the PNAS paper by Alessandro Silvano’s team. She found it documented reversed trends in surface salinity and sea ice since 2015–2016, not a current reversing. The analysis spans 12 years, too short to support a current reversal; such patterns would need hundreds of years of data. After 2015–2016 the Southern Ocean became saltier as sea ice declined, reversing earlier tendencies. A bne IntelliNews article miscast these findings and invoked the AMOC’s Deep Western Boundary Current, and was shared online; the study did not address that current.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Pronghorn antelope have 10x (10 power, or 10 times magnified) vision, which means that on a clear night, they can see the rings of Saturn.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. Dr. Megan Hart, a wildlife-astronomy analyst, evaluated the claim and checked reported magnification figures. She found pronghorn vision is about 8x, not 10x. She also noted that Saturn’s rings generally need roughly 25x to detect and about 50x to see clearly. At 8x, Saturn would present only a small disk with bulges, not distinct rings. Hart added that pronghorn possess exceptional eyesight, with very large eyes and a nearly 300-degree field of view. Those traits do not overcome the magnification gap between 8x and ring visibility thresholds. The claim circulated as a meme in November 2021.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
Some online posts advise storing valuables in a dishwasher during a hurricane.

FACT-CHECKED: FALSE. According to Dr. Erin Caldwell, a home-appliance safety researcher, the claim that a dishwasher is a safe, waterproof place for documents and valuables during a hurricane does not hold up. She reviewed reports describing flooded machines and lost items. She noted that these appliances are designed to get wet inside and are not sealed against water intrusion. Dr. Caldwell also examined how some online posts advise the practice and how a few people reported success. She found hard evidence for reliable protection to be scarce. Her assessment is clear. Dishwashers are not reliable waterproof safes and should not be used for storage in storms.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.

text:
A protein in human saliva called Histatin can help wounds heal faster.

FACT-CHECKED: TRUE. According to Dr. Kara Linden, a wound-healing researcher, the claim that a protein in human saliva called histatin can help wounds heal faster is accurate. She cites findings that histatins, including Histatin-1, promote faster wound repair. Research shows oral wounds heal faster and with less scarring than skin wounds, with saliva a key factor and histatins relevant peptides. Histatins are antimicrobial peptides involved in wound healing. She also notes saliva carries bacteria that can cause infection, so licking wounds is not advised. Risk is higher for deep wounds or vulnerable patients. Use sterile cleaning and seek medical care when needed. Severe infections have occurred after people licked wounds.
user_description: An independent fact-checking website founded in 1994 that investigates urban legends, misinformation, rumors, and viral claims circulating online and in the media.
